Despite their immense importance, coral reefs are being damaged and destroyed due to natural and anthropogenic activities. The major reef formations in India are **restricted to the Gulf of Mannar, Palk bay, Gulf of Kutch, Andaman and Nicobar Islands** **and the Lakshadweep islands**. While the Lakshadweep reefs are atolls, the others are all fringing reefs. Patchy coral is present in the inter-tidal areas of the central west coast of the country. So, the marine biologist should include the following **four** regions in her itinerary, as they have **significant** **natural coral reef formations**: Gulf of Kutch, Gulf of Mannar, Andaman & Nicobar Islands and Lakshadweep Islands. **So, points I, III, IV and V are correct.**
● The Gulf of Khambhat experiences high turbidity, strong tidal currents and heavy sediment load, which are **unfavorable conditions for coral reef development**. **So, point II is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Bitumen
II. 	 Lubricating oil
III. 	 Kerosene
IV. 	 Shale oil
How many of the above products can be produced by the refinement of crude oil?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Petroleum, also called crude oil**, is a naturally occurring liquid found beneath the earth’s surface that can be refined into fuel. After crude oil is removed from the ground, it is sent to a refinery where different parts of the crude oil are separated into usable petroleum products.
● The byproducts obtained by refining crude oil include Petroleum Gas in Liquid form (LPG), Petrol, **Kerosene**, Diesel, **Lubricating oil**, Paraffin wax and **Bitumen**. **So, points I, II and III are correct.**
● **Shale oil** is a high-quality crude oil that lies between layers of shale rock, impermeable mudstone, or siltstone. Oil companies produce shale oil by fracturing the rock formations that contain the layers of oil in a process known as **"fracking.**
○ The difference between shale oil and crude oil is in the method of its extraction. Because shale oil is bound up tightly in impermeable rock, it is considered **tight oil and requires hydraulic** **fracturing to be extracted**. Crude oil can be drilled out conventionally. **So, point IV is not** **correct.** **So, only three of the above products can be produced by the refinement of crude oil.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.questionText:
Consider the following activities:
I. - Trade and commerce
II. 	 Transport
III. 	 Telecommunications
IV. 	 Research and Development
V. 	 Financial and legal consultants
How many of the above are considered tertiary activities?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Tertiary activities** include both **production and exchange.** The production involves the
**‘provision’ of services that are ‘consumed’**. The output is indirectly measured in terms of **wages**
**and salaries**. Exchange involves trade, transport and communication facilities that are used to overcome
distance. **Tertiary activities,** therefore, involve the **commercial output of services rather than**
**the production of tangible goods.** They are not directly involved in the processing of physical raw
materials.
● The following are the examples of tertiary activities: ○ **Trade and commerce:** Trade is essentially buying and selling of items produced elsewhere. All the services in retail and wholesale trading or commerce are specifically intended for profit. The towns and cities where all these works take place are known as trading centres. **So, point I is** **correct.** ○ **Transport:** Transport is a service or facility by which people, materials and manufactured goods are physically carried from one location to another. **So, point II is correct.** ○ **Telecommunications:** The use of telecommunications is linked to the development of modern technology. It has revolutionised communications because of the speed with which messages are sent. The time is reduced from weeks to minutes. Besides, the recent advancements like mobile telephony have made communications direct and instantaneous at any time and from anywhere. **So, point III is correct.**
● **Quaternary activities** involve the collection, production and dissemination of information or even the production of information. Quaternary activities centre around **research, development** and may be seen as an advanced form of services involving specialised knowledge and technical skills. **So, point IV** **is not correct.**
● **Quinary activities** are services that focus on the creation, re-arrangement and interpretation of new and existing ideas; data interpretation and the use and evaluation of new technologies. Often referred to as **‘gold collar’ professions,** they represent another subdivision of the tertiary sector representing special and highly paid skills of senior business executives, government officials, research scientists, **financial and legal consultants, etc**. Their importance in the structure of advanced economies far outweighs their numbers. **So, point V is not correct.** **So, only three of the above are considered tertiary activities.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Transport (Roadways, Railways, Waterways, Ports, Airways, Pipelines, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to National highways in India, Consider the following statements:
I. - NH44 is the country’s longest highway.
II. 	 The strategically important Zojila Tunnel aims to facilitate travel on the NH-1.
III. 	 The Leh-Manali Motorway is the world’s second-highest motorable highway.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Longest Highway in India is NH 44,** spanning 3,745 km from Srinagar (Jammu & Kashmir) to Kanyakumari (Tamil Nadu). This highway was formed by merging seven major national highways from the old numbering system, including NH 1A, NH 1, NH 2, NH 3, NH 75, NH 26, and NH 7. Other major highways include NH 6 (1,873 km), NH 53 (1,781 km), NH 16 (1,711 km), NH 66 (1,622 km), and NH 19 (1,435 km). **So, statement 1 is correct.**
● The Zojila Tunnel project aims to **make travel on the Srinagar-Kargil-Leh section of NH-1** safer by eliminating the threat of avalanches. Strategically located at an altitude of 11,578 feet, it will provide an all-weather connectivity between Srinagar and Ladakh. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The **Leh–Manali Highway** connecting Leh in Ladakh to Manali in Himachal Pradesh is the **world’s** **second highest-altitude motorable highway**. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer**.
**Relevance:** Recently, Karnataka government withdrew its affidavit for complete closure of NH 766 through Bandipur Tiger Reserve, amid protests and support.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. - Sambhar lake
II. 	 Wular lake
III. 	 Lonar lake
How many of the above are saline lakes?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Sambhar lake** is the **largest saltwater lake in India.**  It is located in the districts of **Nagaur and** **Jaipur in Rajasthan.** This saline wetland is surrounded by the Aravalli hills. The water from two major ephemeral streams, namely **Mendha and Runpangarh,** along with numerous rivulets and surface runoff, feed the lake. **So, point I is correct.**
● **Wular lake,** located at the foothills of the **Haramuk Mountain in Jammu & Kashmir**, is the **second-largest freshwater lake in Asia.** The lake is situated between the towns of Sopore and Bandipora, near Sangrama along the Baramulla Road. The primary source of water for Wular Lake is the **Jhelum River. So, point II is not correct.**
● **Lonar lake** is a **saline and alkaline lake** located in **Maharashtra State**. It was created due to a meteorite. It is the only known saline crater lake in the world, formed by the impact of a meteorite about 50,000 years ago. **Its water is seven times saltier than seawater.** One of the most striking aspects is its color, which changes from green to pink depending on the season and the water conditions. This is caused by the presence of microorganisms that thrive in the saline and alkaline environment of the lake. **So, point III is correct.** **So, only two of the above are saline lakes in India.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: Which one of the following sectors in India is the largest consumer of groundwater?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Dynamic Ground Water Resources of India** are periodically assessed through a joint effort by the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** and respective **State Governments.** According to the **latest assessment in 2022,** the Annual Extractable Ground Water Resource in the country stands at **398 billion cubic metres (BCM).**
● Of this, the Annual Ground Water Extraction for all uses amounts to 239.16 BCM, with approximately
**208.49 BCM (87%)** being utilised for **agricultural activities alone.** The availability and extraction of groundwater depend on multiple interrelated factors, including the intensity and duration of rainfall, the geological characteristics of the region, the number and effectiveness of recharge structures and the demand for groundwater across sectors such as industry, domestic use and agriculture. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: With reference to petrology, ‘‘lithification’’ is the process of

options.a: Forcing the rocks to lower levels by tectonic processes
options.b: Conversion of unconsolidated sediments into sedimentary rocks
options.c: Hot magma getting converted into solid rock
options.d: Reorganisation of the original minerals within rocks due to crushing

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Lithification** is the process of **converting unconsolidated sediments into sedimentary rock.** Lithification involves primarily the processes of **compaction and cementation.** Rocks (igneous, sedimentary and metamorphic) of the earth’s surface are exposed to denudational agents, and are broken up into various sizes of fragments. Such fragments are transported by different exogenous agencies and deposited. These deposits through compaction turn into rocks. This process is called lithification.
● **Metamorphism** occurs when rocks are forced down to lower levels by tectonic processes or when molten magma rising through the crust comes in contact with the crustal rocks or the underlying rocks are subjected to great amounts of pressure by overlying rocks.
● **Crystallisation** is the process of conversion of hot liquid magma into solid rocks. Igneous rocks are formed in this manner.
● Mechanical disruption and reorganisation of the original minerals within rocks due to breaking and crushing without any appreciable chemical changes is called **dynamic metamorphism.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the drainage basins in India:
I. - Both the Himalayan and peninsular rivers have attained their base level of erosion.
II. 	 Himalayan rivers have a perennial flow, whereas Peninsular rivers have a seasonal flow.
III. 	 Himalayan rivers are in their youthful stage, whereas the Peninsular rivers have reached a mature stage of development.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The drainage pattern or system of an area refers to the system of flow of surface water mainly through the rivers and basins forms. The drainage system is related to a number of factors, for example slope of land, geological structure, amount of volume of water and velocity of water.
● The Himalayas play an important role in the drainage system of North India. **Himalayan rivers** differ from those of South India as they are **still deepening their valleys rather rapidly.** The debris eroded by these rivers is carried to the plains and seas and deposited there. The **Great North Indian plain** has been formed by the **silt brought down by these rivers.** Some of the **Himalayan rivers are** **older than the Himalayas themselves.** As the ranges of the Himalayas had been rising upwards, these rivers were equally busy in downward cutting, forming deep gorges and valleys.
● The Peninsular India is an ancient landmass. Therefore, the **streams flowing through this region are** **in their old stage.** They have almost attained their base level of erosion. Their capacity to **erode** **valleys vertically has almost come to a negligible stage.** Now these streams are eroding their sides at a slow pace. This is resulting in the broadening of their valleys. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The rivers which have their origin in the **Himalayas are perennial.** These rivers are fed by the **melting of ice and snow lying near the tongue of glaciers** of the Great Himalayan Range (Himadri). In the rivers of South India, the flow of water is highly fluctuating. While the rivers are in **spate during** **the monsoons,** they are almost dry during the long rainless months. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Himalayan rivers** are in their **youthful stage,** characterised by active vertical erosion that results in the **formation of deep valleys and gorges**. Notable examples include the Indus gorge near Bunji in Jammu & Kashmir, which reaches a depth of about 5,200 meters, as well as similar gorges formed by the Sutlej and Brahmaputra rivers. In contrast, **Peninsular rivers** are in their mature to old stage, having **largely achieved their base level of erosion** and thus exhibit broader, more stable valleys with less vertical cutting. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
With reference to the cropping system in India, consider the following statements:
I. - Multiple-cropping is the practice of growing two or more crops simultaneously on the same piece of land with a definite row pattern.
II. 	 Inter-cropping is the practice of growing two or more crops on the same piece of land within a single calendar year.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In India, the cropping pattern follows two distinct seasons: **Kharif season** from **July to October** and **Rabi season from October to March**. The crops grown between **March to June** are called **zaid.** The crops are grown solo or mixed (mixed-cropping) or in a definite sequence (rotational cropping). The land may be occupied by one crop during one session (mono cropping) or by two crops during one season (doublecropping), which may be grown in a year in a sequence.
● **Inter-cropping:** It is the practice of **growing two or more crops simultaneously on the same** **piece of land with a definite row pattern.** For example, growing setaria and redgram in a 5:1 ratio. Thus, cropping intensity in the spatial dimension is achieved. Inter-cropping was originally practiced as an insurance against crop failure under rainfed conditions. At present, the main objective of intercropping is higher productivity per unit area in addition to stability in production. **So, statement I is** **not correct.**
● **Multiple-cropping:** It refers to the **practice of growing two or more crops on the same piece** **of land in one calendar year**. It is an intensification of cropping in time and space dimensions, i.e., a greater number of crops within a year and a greater number of crops on the same piece of land at any given period. It includes intercropping, mixed-cropping and sequence cropping. Double-cropping is a case where the land is occupied by two crops, which are grown in a year in sequence. **So, statement II is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Some other types of cropping patterns:**
● **Mixed-cropping:** It is the growing of two or more crops simultaneously intermingled without any row pattern. It is a common practice in most of the dryland tracts of India. Seeds of different crops are mixed in a certain proportion and are sown.
● **Sequence-Cropping:** It can be defined as growing two or more crops in a sequence on the same piece of land in a farming year. Depending on the number of crops grown in a year, it is called double, triple or quadruple cropping, involving two, three and four crops respectively.
● **Integrated Farming System:** It is a holistic method of combining several enterprises like cropping system, dairy, piggery, poultry, fishery, bee-keeping, etc., in a harmonious way so as to complement each other. The objective is efficient resource utilisation and maximisation of profit in such a way as to cause the least damage to soil and environment.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
In India, the problem of soil erosion is associated with which of the following?
I. - Extensive irrigation
II. 	 Excessive canal seepage
III. 	 Excessive grazing
IV. 	 Terrace farming
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● A major factor responsible for the **degradation of natural resources is soil erosion.** It has been estimated that accelerated soil erosion has irreversibly destroyed some 430 mha of land area covering **30% of the present cultivated area** in different countries of the world. The loss of topsoil resulting in reduced productivity is a most serious degradation problem in the Indian sub-continent.
● **Erosion by water** is the most serious degradation problem in the Indian Context. At present, soil erosion is taking place at a rate of **16.35 tons per hectare per year** totaling 5334 million tons per year. Nearly **29% of the total eroded soil** is permanently lost to the sea and nearly 10% is deposited in reservoirs.
● A large fraction of **irrigation has been achieved through the expansion of the canal irrigated**
**area.** In almost all cases, the groundwater table which was several meters deep before the introduction
of irrigation, has been rising following the introduction of irrigation. When the groundwater table reaches
within 2 meters of the surface, it contributes significantly to evaporation from the soil surface and causes
soil salinisation. **So, point I is correct.**
● Another cause of **soil degradation in irrigated areas is waterlogging due to excessive water**
**application** and **canal seepage.** It not only hampers crop growth but also degrades the soil and
productivity is reduced considerably.  **So, point II is correct.**
● **Wind erosion** is a serious problem in the Arid and Semi-arid regions, including the states of Rajasthan,
Haryana, Gujarat and Punjab. Removal of natural vegetative cover resulting from **excessive grazing**
**and extension of agriculture to marginal areas** is the major human-induced factor leading to
accelerated wind erosion.  **So, point III is correct.**
● **Terrace Farming** is a **soil conservation technique** and not a cause of soil erosion. It involves
**creating stepped levels on hill slopes** to **reduce surface runoff and prevent soil loss**. Instead
of contributing to erosion, terrace farming helps control it, especially in hilly and mountainous regions
by slowing down water flow and allowing better water absorption into the soil. **So, point IV is not**
**correct.**
**Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - It is a Kharif crop.
II. 	 It requires a minimum of 210 frost-free days in a year.
III. 	 Gujarat, Maharashtra and Telangana are its leading producers.
Which is this crop?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Cotton is one of the most **important fiber and cash** **crops** of India and plays a dominant role in the industrial and agricultural economy of the country. In India, there are ten major cotton-growing States which are divided into three zones, viz. the **north zone, the central** **zone and the south zone.** India is the **only country** in the world where **all four cultivated species** are grown on a commercial scale.
● **It is a Kharif Crop that grows in tropical and** **subtropical regions**. India supports the growth of both short staple (Indian) and long staple (American) cotton, called Narma, in the northwestern regions of the country**. It grows in areas with a minimum of 210** **frost-free days in a year**. It grows well in black soils of Deccan and Malwa plateau and also in Satluj-Ganga plain, having red and laterite soils of the peninsular region. **Leading producers of this crop are Gujarat, Maharashtra and Telangana.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Transport (Roadways, Railways, Waterways, Ports, Airways, Pipelines, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to the Inland Waterways in India:
I. - National Waterway 1 passes through the Indo-Gangetic alluvial plain.
II. 	 National Waterway 2 comprises the Sadiya– Dhubri stretch of the Brahmaputra river.
III. 	 National Waterway 4 is the longest inland waterway of India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Ganga - Bhagirathi - Hooghly river system was declared as National Waterway-1 (NW-1) in 1986. It is the longest inland waterway of India, spanning 1,620 km. This waterway stretches from Prayagraj (Allahabad) in Uttar Pradesh to Haldia in West Bengal, a region that is characterized by the vast, fertile Indo-Gangetic plains. **So, statement I is correct and statement III is not correct.**
● The river Brahmaputra having a length of 891 Km between Bangladesh Border and Sadiya was declared as National Waterway no. 2 in 1988. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. - Tamil Nadu is the most urbanised State in India, followed by Maharashtra.
II. 	 Delhi is the most urbanised Union Territory in India, followed by Chandigarh.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **c**
**Explanation:** According to SBI Research, India's urban population, which was 31.1% in the 2011 Census, is projected to rise to 35–37% in the recent Census, indicating a rapidly urbanising trend.
● According to SBI Research, **Tamil Nadu continues to be the most urbanised state in India**, with **54% of its population** living in urban areas. It is followed by **Maharashtra,** which has **48.8% of its** **population** in urban areas and leads in terms of the absolute number of urban residents. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● As per Census 2011, **Delhi recorded an exceptionally high level of urbanisation, with 97.5% of**
**its total population living in urban areas**, making it the most urbanised **Union Territory in India.**
This reflects Delhi's status as the national capital and a major metropolitan hub with extensive
infrastructure, employment opportunities and immigration from other states. **Chandigarh,** another
Union Territory, ranked **second in terms of urbanisation** among Union Territories, **with 97.25%**
**of its population** residing in urban areas. Chandigarh’s high degree of urbanisation is attributed to its
administrative significance, urban planning and relatively compact geography. **So, statement II is**
**correct.**
**Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** The Indian Census, which is conducted every 10 years, has been delayed and is now expected to begin in 2025.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Transport (Roadways, Railways, Waterways, Ports, Airways, Pipelines, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements is/are correct with regard to the major ports of India?
I. - Vishakhapatnam is the deepest landlocked port.
II. 	 Paradwip port is specialised in the export of iron ore.
III. 	 Shyama Prasad Mookerjee Port is a tidal inland riverine port.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **d**
**Explanation:**
● India, with its long coastline of over **7,500 km**, relies heavily on its ports for **international trade,** **energy imports and economic connectivity.** The country has a network of **13 major ports** and around **200 minor ports**, which together handle more than 90% of India’s external trade by volume.
● **Visakhapatnam Port**, located on the east coast of India in Andhra Pradesh, is the **deepest** **landlocked and well-protected port in the country**. Despite being landlocked, it has direct access to the Bay of Bengal through a natural harbour. Its strategic location and natural depth make it ideal for handling large vessels and its protection from cyclones and high tides enhances its reliability for maritime trade throughout the year. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Paradwip Port,** situated on the coast of Odisha, is one of the major ports of India and plays **a crucial** **role in the export of iron ore**, especially to countries like China and Japan. Its proximity to the mineral-rich states of Odisha, Jharkhand and Chhattisgarh gives it an advantage in serving as a key outlet for bulk cargo exports, particularly iron ore, coal, and other raw materials essential for heavy industries. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **Shyama Prasad Mookerjee Port, formerly known as Kolkata Port, is the only tidal inland** **riverine port in India**. It is located along the **Hooghly River** and is heavily dependent on the tidal flow for navigation. Due to continuous siltation, the port requires **regular dredging to maintain** **navigability for cargo vessels**. Despite these challenges, it remains vital for trade in eastern and northeastern India, including neighbouring countries like Bhutan and Nepal. **So, statement III is** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** In recent years, the Government of India has been modernising and expanding port infrastructure under various initiatives.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.microTopic: Environmental and Global Issues (Climate Change, Drought, ZBNF, WTO, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Match the following:
Type of Drought Characteristic
I. - Meteorological A.
Caused by a significant decrease in rainfall compared to the normal average
II. - Hydrological B.
Results from prolonged meteorological drought affecting surface and groundwater
III. - Agricultural C.
Leads to soil moisture deficit and impacts crop growth and productivity

options.a: I - A, II - B, III - C
options.b: I - B, II - C, III - A
options.c: I - C, II - B, III - A
options.d: I - B, II - A, III - C

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Drought** is an extended period of **unusually dry weather** with insufficient rainfall for the cultivation of crops. Droughts can last for months or years and can have a large-scale impact on human society as well as the ecosystem of the region. Drought is therefore a region and crop-specific phenomenon. It may result in a situation of distress caused by a lack of rainfall. Drought has therefore been classified as:
○ **Meteorological drought:** It is the earliest form of drought, **defined by a significant** **decrease in precipitation over a specific region and period**, typically compared to longterm average values. It does not take into account water usage or storage and serves as a trigger for other types of droughts. ○ **Hydrological drought:** It is a consequence of prolonged meteorological drought. **It occurs** **when surface water bodies (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and groundwater levels fall** **below average** due to insufficient rainfall over time. It impacts water availability for domestic use, power generation, and industry.
○ **Agricultural drought:** **It happens when soil moisture becomes inadequate** to support healthy crop growth, usually during critical stages of crop development. It may result from meteorological drought or inefficient irrigation practices and directly affect food security and farmers' livelihoods. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Hydrological droughts are further classified as under:**
● **Surface water drought:** It is concerned with the drying up of surface water resources such as rivers, streams, lakes, ponds and other forms of surface reservoirs. Most of the surface droughts are caused due to anthropogenic activities like deforestation, construction of roads, buildings and mining activities, etc.
● **Groundwater drought:** It is associated with a decrease in groundwater content. A fall in the level of groundwater may be due to excessive pumping of groundwater by industries or for increasing agricultural production.
**Relevance:** A new assessment report says that over 60% of Indian districts are high climate risk zones prone to flood and drought, the two commonest natural hazards.

questions.subject: Agriculture
questions.microTopic: Allied Sectors (Animal Husbandry, Horticulture, Sericulture, Apiculture, Aquaculture)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the fisheries sector in India, consider the following statements:
I. - India is the second largest fish producing country in the world.
II. 	 Andhra Pradesh is the largest fish producer in the country followed by Gujarat.
III. 	 Around 70% of India’s fish production comes from inland waters.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer:** **d**
**Explanation:**
● India is the **second largest fish producing country** with around 8% share in global fish production.
Over the past two decades, India's fisheries sector has witnessed significant growth and transformation.
From technological advancements to policy reforms, the period from 2004 to 2024 has been marked by
milestones that have bolstered India's position in global fisheries and aquaculture. **So, statement I is**
**correct.**
● India contributes about 7% to the global fish production. Andhra Pradesh is the largest fish producer in
the country followed by West Bengal and Gujarat. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Around 70% of India’s fish production comes from inland waters, of which nearly 65% comes from
aquaculture. Out of the total inland aquaculture production, Indian Major Carps are the most cultured
freshwater fish followed by Exotic Carps, Minor Carps, Catfish and Trout. **So, statement III is**
**correct.**
**Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Relevance:** A FAO Report states that the global fisheries and aquaculture production reached a new record
high.

questions.subject: Geography
questions.questionText: These settlements are fragmented into several units physically separated from each other. These units are locally called panna, para, palli, nagla, and dhani in India. This is the most likely description of:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Human Settlement** means a **cluster of dwellings** of any type or size where human beings live. For this purpose, people may erect houses and other structures and command some area or territory as their economic support base.
● The process involves **grouping of people** and **apportioning of territory** as a **resource base**. Settlements vary in **size and type**, ranging from **hamlets to metropolitan cities**.
● With size, the **economic character**, **social structure**, **ecology**, and **technology** of settlements also change. They may be **small and sparsely spaced** or **large and closely spaced**. Sparsely located small settlements are called **villages**, often engaged in **agriculture** or **primary activities**.
● **Hamleted Settlements:** ○ In certain regions of India, settlements are fragmented into **multiple physically separated** **units,** yet they share a common village name. These smaller units are locally known by terms such as **panna, para, palli, nagla, dhani, etc.,** depending on the region. The fragmentation is often influenced by social, cultural, or ethnic factors, leading to spatial separation within a single community. Such hamleted settlements are commonly found in the **middle and lower Ganga** **plains, Chhattisgarh and the lower Himalayan valleys.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Clustered Settlements:** The clustered rural settlement is a compact or closely builtup area of houses. In this type of village, the general living area is distinct and separated from the surrounding farms, barns and pastures.
● **Semi-Clustered Settlements:** Semi-clustered or fragmented settlements may result from the tendency of clustering in a restricted area of dispersed settlement. More often, such a pattern may also result from the segregation or fragmentation of a large, compact village.
● **Dispersed Settlements**: Dispersed or isolated settlement patterns in India appear in the **form of isolated huts or hamlets** of a few huts in remote jungles, or on small hills with **farms or pasture on the slopes.** Extreme dispersion of settlement is often caused by the extremely fragmented nature of the terrain and land resource base of habitable areas.
